sourceMovie.title: Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time works as a animation movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The animation and action blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Evangelion: 1.0 You Are (Not) Alone
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time compelling — the animation, the action — shows up in Evangelion: 1.0 You Are (Not) Alone (2007) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that uplifting storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Evangelion: 2.0 You Can (Not) Advance
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The animation energy of Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time is alive in Evangelion: 2.0 You Can (Not) Advance (2009), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a uplifting payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Evangelion: 3.0 You Can (Not) Redo
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Evangelion: 3.0 You Can (Not) Redo (2012) operates in the same animation as Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The uplifting payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Belle
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Belle (2021) belongs to the same conversation as Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time — both anchor their animation in science-fiction emotion that doesn't let go. The uplifting undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Ghost in the Shell 2.0
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time's animation stayed with you, Ghost in the Shell 2.0 (2008) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The action through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Appleseed Alpha
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time excels in animation, Appleseed Alpha (2014) doubles down on action to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Mazinger Z: Infinity
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Mazinger Z: Infinity (2017) is the natural next step after Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time: same animation DNA, different perspective. The uplifting streak and action elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Paprika
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time who respond to its animation will find Paprika (2006) immediately familiar. Both films share a science-fiction sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Metropolis
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time fans keep landing on Metropolis (2001) — the animation is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: A Boy and His Dog
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: A Boy and His Dog draws from the same science-fiction well as Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time, channeling drama in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 1975 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

faq.question: What should I watch after Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time?
faq.answer: Try Evangelion: 1.0 You Are (Not) Alone, Evangelion: 2.0 You Can (Not) Advance, Evangelion: 3.0 You Can (Not) Redo—each shares DNA with Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time?
faq.answer: Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time (2021) is best described as Animation, Action, Science Fiction, Drama. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Evangelion: 3.0+1.0 Thrice Upon a Time part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Rebuild of Evangelion Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
